nativeRange.habitat: Low-altitude, slow-moving or standing blackwater streams and tributaries, often tannin-stained and shaded by overhanging vegetation, with leaf litter, submerged wood, and soft, acidic water.
nativeRange.biotope: Coastal blackwater drainages of the Guianas and Venezuelan Orinoco delta; also recorded from Trinidad.

diet.notes: Omnivorous forager that has been documented eating small crustaceans, insect larvae, and detritus in the wild. In captivity generally accepts quality cichlid pellets and flake foods alongside frozen or live bloodworm, Artemia, daphnia, and finely chopped vegetables. A varied diet tends to support the best coloration and conditioning.

avoidWith: very small dwarf shrimp (may be eaten)
highly active tankmates that bully shy fish off food

summary: The Keyhole Cichlid (Cleithracara maronii) is widely regarded as one of the most community-friendly cichlids available, named for the distinctive keyhole-shaped marking on its flank. Native to slow-moving blackwater streams of the Guianas, it is a strong choice for planted, soft-water South American community tanks.
careNotes: Generally considered one of the most peaceful cichlids in the hobby and a strong candidate for soft-water South American community tanks. Tends to be shy, especially when newly introduced, and have been reported to lose color and refuse food when stressed by boisterous tankmates or bright open setups — subdued lighting, floating plants, driftwood, and leaf litter help them settle. A long footprint (around 90 cm or more) is generally advised over tall tanks, as adults appreciate open mid-water swimming space framed by shelter. Forms strong, generally lifelong monogamous pair bonds; buying a small group of juveniles (6 or so) and allowing a pair to form, then rehoming the surplus, is a commonly recommended route. Sexual dimorphism is moderate — adult males have been reported to grow noticeably larger than females and to develop extended dorsal and anal-fin filaments. The species' native blackwater habitat suggests it tends to do best in soft, slightly acidic water with tannin staining, though it is broadly adaptable.
breedingNotes: Biparental, monogamous substrate spawner. Pairs typically clean a flat rock, broad plant leaf, or piece of driftwood and deposit up to several hundred eggs. Eggs have been reported to hatch in approximately 2–5 days, with fry becoming free-swimming a few days later. Both parents actively guard eggs, wrigglers, and free-swimming fry, and parental care has been reported to continue for several months — unusually long among cichlids. Fry can be started on microworms or baby brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding is generally considered straightforward once a bonded pair is established and water quality is stable.

primary.name: Seriously Fish
primary.notes: Aquarium Co-Op product page (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/products/keyhole-cichlid) returned HTTP 404; promoted Seriously Fish from secondary to primary per playbook fallback. Source of native range, taxonomy, water parameters, sizing, breeding behaviour, and minimum footprint.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~10 cm), temperature (22–27°C), pH (6.0–7.2), lifespan (7–10 years), monogamous pairing, and breeding behaviour. Recommends 55 US gal (~208 L) minimum — used as the upper anchor for the tank-size compromise.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (10–12 cm), temperature (24–27°C), peaceful temperament, omnivorous diet, native blackwater habitat (French Guiana, Suriname), and biparental egg-guarding behaviour. pH range listed as 6.6–8.0 is wider on the alkaline end than other sources; retained the more commonly cited 6.0–7.5 range that better matches the wild blackwater habitat.

fish.breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner that generally pairs and spawns without intervention once a bonded pair has formed and water quality is stable. Extended parental care of fry is a notable feature. Pair formation from a juvenile group is the most reliable route.
